profile.summary: A mid-level Product Designer owns end-to-end design work for parts of a product, from framing flows and prototyping to shipping polished UI with engineers and product managers. They turn user and business needs into usable experiences, contribute to research and discovery, and improve designs through feedback, data, and iteration. Scope is usually a team or feature area rather than product-wide direction.

known_challenges.text: Balancing speed to ship with enough discovery, testing, and iteration.
Handling feedback from product, engineering, and other designers without losing clarity or confidence.
Working through vague problem statements or shifting priorities.
Keeping quality high when design debt and edge cases pile up.
Sustaining energy when deadlines compress craft time and create constant context switching.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Clarity of problem framing and priorities
recommended_question_themes.why: Mid-level designers do better work when the problem, constraints, and success signals are clear enough to design against.
They often sit in the middle of many opinions, so it matters whether feedback is actionable and decisions get made in time.
This level is usually stretching from executing screens to owning flows, trade-offs, and delivery with less hand-holding.
Strong day-to-day collaboration shapes scope, feasibility, and whether good design actually ships.
Design quality drops when they are rushing across too many tickets or never get uninterrupted time to think.

terminology.meaning: The steps a person takes to complete a task.
A low-detail layout used to explore structure and flow.
A clickable model used to test or explain behavior.
Shared components, patterns, and rules for product design.
Designing so people with different needs can use it.
Structured feedback focused on the work, not the person.
Early work to understand problems, users, and opportunities.
Sharing final design details needed for engineering to build.
Improving a design through repeated changes and feedback.
Working closely with product, engineering, and other partners.
A less common scenario the design still needs to handle.
Choosing what to optimize when not everything can fit.
The boundaries of what the design work will cover.
Limits like time, tech, policy, or business needs.
Signals used to judge whether a design worked.

listen_for: Whether they talk about user problems or jump straight to screens.
How clearly they explain trade-offs between user needs, business goals, and engineering limits.
Whether feedback sounds collaborative, blocked, or overly approval-driven.
Signs they are shipping iteratively versus waiting for perfect designs.
Whether they mention data, research, or testing when judging design quality.
avoid: Treating them like a pixel-polisher instead of a partner in product decisions.
Assuming faster delivery always means less design process.
Giving vague feedback like "make it cleaner" without naming the problem.
Pushing them to own product strategy that should sit beyond mid-level scope.
